3:0.rendering: Look, you are beautiful, my love. Look, you are beautiful. Your eyes are doves behind your veil. Your hair is like a flock of goats streaming down from Mount Gilead.
3:0.note: In the original Hebrew, the word for "beautiful" (yafah) is repeated twice for emphasis, creating a rhythm of delighted wonder. This poetic description of the beloved's eyes as doves and her hair as flowing goats draws on ancient Near Eastern imagery of abundance and peace, part of the Song's larger theme of human love as a return to the garden-like intimacy God intended for his image-bearers, a thread that echoes forward into the New Testament's celebration of covenant love between Christ and his people.

3:1.rendering: Your teeth are like a flock of shorn ewes coming up from the washing, each one has its twin, and not one of them is missing.
3:1.note: The Hebrew word for "flock" (eder) echoes the same term used for God's people as his treasured flock throughout the prophets, while the image of perfectly matched, washed ewes emerging from the water picks up on themes of purity and covenant wholeness. This intimate portrait of the beloved's beauty weaves into the Bible's larger story of God delighting in his people as a flawless, unified community, a thread that finds its ultimate expression in Jesus presenting his bride without spot or wrinkle.

3:2.rendering: Your lips are like a scarlet thread, and your mouth is lovely. Your cheeks are like halves of a pomegranate behind your veil.
3:2.note: The Hebrew word for "scarlet thread" (hut shani) echoes the cord Rahab hung from her window in Joshua 2, a signal of rescue and covenant faithfulness. Here the lover's gaze celebrates the beauty of his bride's veiled face, inviting us into the Song's larger theme of intimate, exclusive love that mirrors God's delight in his people and ultimately finds its fulfillment in Jesus the bridegroom.

3:3.rendering: Your neck is like the tower of David, built in rows of stone; a thousand shields hang on it, all of them shields of warriors.
3:3.note: The Hebrew word for "neck" here carries the idea of strength and beauty, echoing the tower imagery that links back to the fortified cities in the story of Israel. This love poem celebrates the beloved's neck as both elegant and battle-ready, a picture of secure beauty that quietly foreshadows how the Song's garden-like intimacy points forward to the ultimate union of heaven and earth in Jesus and his bride.

3:4.rendering: Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle, that graze among the lilies.
3:4.note: In the lush garden imagery of Song of Solomon, the lover's description of his beloved's breasts as twin gazelle fawns grazing among lilies echoes the Eden-like setting where heaven and earth overlapped in delight and abundance, a theme that threads through the Bible's story of covenant love and finds its ultimate echo in the New Testament's celebration of Christ and his bride.

3:5.rendering: Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, I will go to the mountain of myrrh and to the hill of frankincense.
3:5.note: The lover's promise to come at dawn to the "mountain of myrrh" and "hill of frankincense" carries a double meaning in Hebrew: these fragrant spices (mor and levonah) evoke both the erotic intimacy of the garden and the sacred incense burned in the tabernacle and temple. This blends the language of human love with the language of worship, an early hint that the whole Song is participating in the larger biblical theme of heaven and earth overlapping, where covenant love between a man and woman echoes God's desire to dwell with his people, a thread that runs all the way to Jesus, the bridegroom who makes his people a fragrant offering.

3:6.rendering: You are altogether beautiful, my love. There is no flaw in you.
3:6.note: The Hebrew word for "flaw" (mum) is the same term used for physical defects that disqualified an animal from being offered in the temple or a priest from serving, yet here the lover sees his beloved as completely perfect. This love song echoes the creation story's declaration of "very good" and points forward to how Jesus presents his bride, the church, as spotless and without blemish before God.

3:7.rendering: Come with me from Lebanon, my bride. Come with me from Lebanon. Descend from the peak of Amana, from the peak of Senir and Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards.
3:7.note: The Hebrew word for "come" here is the same one used when God calls to his people to return from exile, and the dangerous mountain peaks echo the imagery of Eden's guarded heights. This invitation pulls the bride down from wild, untamed places into the safety of covenant love, a thread that runs through the prophets' promises of restored intimacy between God and his people, ultimately fulfilled in Jesus calling his bride out of darkness into his marvelous light.

3:8.rendering: You have stolen my heart, my sister, my bride. You have stolen my heart with one glance of your eyes, with one jewel from your necklace.
3:8.note: The Hebrew word for "stolen my heart" (libabtini) literally means "you have hearted me," an intense expression of being captivated that echoes the garden imagery of the Song and the mutual delight between lovers. This intimate, exclusive language of "sister, my bride" foreshadows the New Testament's portrayal of the church as Christ's beloved bride, where heaven and earth overlap in covenant love.

3:9.rendering: How beautiful is your love, my sister, my bride. How much better is your love than wine, and the fragrance of your oils than all spices.
3:9.note: The word for "love" here is dodim, which echoes the sensual delight of the garden in Eden and the rich, intoxicating aroma of covenant offerings throughout the Torah, inviting us to see this intimate celebration of human love as a thread that runs all the way to the ultimate wedding feast of the Lamb.

3:10.rendering: Your lips drip honey, my bride. Honey and milk are under your tongue. The scent of your garments is like the scent of Lebanon.
3:10.note: The Hebrew word for "honey" (devash) echoes the description of the promised land flowing with milk and honey, while the fragrant garments recall the temple's incense and the cedar forests of Lebanon that supplied wood for God's house, all woven together in this intimate portrait of the bride as a living garden where heaven and earth meet.

3:11.rendering: A locked garden is my sister, my bride; a locked spring, a sealed fountain.
3:11.note: The Hebrew word for "locked" (na'ul) carries the sense of something bolted shut for protection, echoing the garden imagery of Eden where heaven and earth once overlapped in perfect intimacy. This verse weaves into the Bible's larger story of covenant love, where the bride is both fiercely protected and yet an inviting source of life, a pattern that finds its surprising fulfillment in Jesus as the bridegroom who unlocks what sin had sealed.

3:12.rendering: Your channel is an orchard of pomegranates, with choice fruits, henna with nard,
3:12.note: The Hebrew word for "channel" or "shoot" (shelach) echoes the word for "sent one" or "apostle," painting the beloved as a lush garden paradise, an image that reaches back to Eden's trees and forward to Jesus as the true vine whose life produces abundant fruit in his people.

3:13.rendering: nard and saffron, calamus and cinnamon, with all trees of frankincense, myrrh and aloes, with all the finest spices.
3:13.note: This fragrant catalog echoes the spices of the tabernacle and temple incense, but here the garden itself has become a living sanctuary of aromatic delight. The Hebrew word for "myrrh" (mor) carries the same root as the name "Moriah," the mountain where Abraham offered Isaac and where the temple would later stand, quietly threading this love song into the Bible's larger story of a God who desires to dwell with his people in a restored Eden.

3:14.rendering: A garden fountain, a well of living water, and streams flowing from Lebanon.
3:14.note: The Hebrew word for "living water" (mayim chayyim) echoes the life-giving streams of Eden in Genesis 2 and the river that will flow from the renewed temple in Ezekiel 47, portraying the beloved as a restored paradise where heaven and earth meet in overflowing abundance.

3:15.rendering: Awake, O north wind, and come, O south wind! Blow upon my garden, that its spices may flow out. Let my beloved come to his garden, and eat its choicest fruits.
3:15.note: In the Song's lush garden imagery, the woman invites the winds to stir her fragrant spices, echoing Eden's original garden where heaven and earth overlapped in delight. This invitation to her beloved to come and enjoy the fruit carries forward the Bible's longing for covenant love that is freely given, a thread that finds its ultimate echo in Jesus as the bridegroom who enters the garden of his people.

6:0.rendering: How beautiful are your feet in sandals, O prince's daughter. The curves of your thighs are like jewels, the work of a master craftsman's hands.
6:0.note: The Hebrew word for "curves" echoes the term for the rounded borders of the tabernacle curtains, portraying the beloved's body as sacred, skillfully crafted space.

6:1.rendering: Your navel is a rounded bowl that never lacks mixed wine. Your belly is a heap of wheat, encircled with lilies.
6:1.note: The Hebrew word for "navel" can also mean "belly button" or subtly hint at "womb," painting her as a source of intoxicating life and fertile abundance.

6:2.rendering: Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle.
6:2.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "twins" echoes the garden imagery of mutual delight from earlier in the Song, celebrating the lovers' bodies as a shared, fertile paradise.

6:3.rendering: Your neck is like a tower of ivory. Your eyes are pools in Heshbon by the gate of Bath-rabbim. Your nose is like the tower of Lebanon overlooking Damascus.
6:3.note: The verse piles on architectural and geographical metaphors that echo the earlier descriptions of the woman as a fortified city, blending intimacy with strength in ancient Near Eastern love poetry.

6:4.rendering: Your head crowns you like Mount Carmel, and the flowing locks of your head are like purple threads, a king is held captive by your tresses.
6:4.note: The Hebrew word for "purple" echoes the rare royal dye reserved for kings, turning the lover's hair into a captivating banner that even a monarch cannot escape.

6:5.rendering: How beautiful you are, how delightful, my love, with all your charms.
6:5.note: The Hebrew word for "delights" echoes the garden's pleasures in Eden, painting this intimate admiration as a return to creation's original joy.

6:6.rendering: Your stature is like a palm tree, and your breasts are like its clusters of fruit.
6:6.note: The Hebrew word for "stature" (qomah) evokes height and dignity, while the palm tree (tamar) echoes the name of David's daughter and carries echoes of the fertile, upright trees in the garden of Eden.

6:7.rendering: I said, I will climb the palm tree, I will grasp its branches. Let your breasts be like clusters of the vine, and the fragrance of your breath like apples.
6:7.note: The lover's bold resolve to "climb" the palm echoes the garden's invitation to take and eat, a recurring image of delight from Eden onward.

7:0.rendering: If only you were like a brother to me, one who nursed at my mother's breasts. Then if I found you outside, I could kiss you, and no one would despise me.
7:0.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "brother" and "kiss" (ach and nashaq) highlights the tension between public honor and private passion in ancient Near Eastern culture.

7:1.rendering: I would lead you, I would bring you into my mother's house, she who taught me. I would give you spiced wine to drink, the juice of my pomegranate.
7:1.note: The mother’s house was the ancient place of betrothal and wedding preparation, an intimate echo of Ruth 3 where Naomi orchestrates the same space for Ruth and Boaz.

7:2.rendering: His left hand is under my head, and his right hand embraces me.
7:2.note: This intimate posture echoes the exact words of 2:6, creating a refrain that bookends their passionate encounters.

7:3.rendering: I charge you, daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles or by the does of the field: do not stir up or awaken love until it pleases.
7:3.note: The oath by wild gazelles echoes the Hebrew wordplay on "love" and "gazelle," inviting us to respect love's untamed timing.

7:4.rendering: Who is this coming up from the wilderness, leaning on her beloved? Under the apricot tree I awakened you. There your mother conceived you, there she who bore you conceived you.
7:4.note: The Hebrew word for "awakened" echoes the garden's first stirrings of love, linking this desert return to the original paradise of desire.

7:5.rendering: Set me as a seal upon your heart, as a seal upon your arm. For love is as strong as death, its jealousy as unyielding as the grave. Its flashes are flashes of fire, the very flame of the Lord.
7:5.note: The Hebrew word for "jealousy" here is the same one used for God's passionate covenant love in Exodus 34:14, turning human eros into an echo of divine intensity.

7:6.rendering: Many waters cannot quench love, rivers cannot sweep it away. If a man gave all the wealth of his house for love, they would utterly despise him.
7:6.note: The Hebrew word for "despise" carries the force of "utterly scorn" or treat as nothing, showing love's priceless nature that no wealth can purchase.

7:7.rendering: We have a little sister, and she has no breasts. What shall we do for our sister on the day when she is spoken for?
7:7.note: The brothers' anxious planning for their flat-chested little sister echoes the protective worry of ancient family structures guarding a girl's marriage prospects.

7:8.rendering: If she is a wall, we will build on her a battlement of silver. But if she is a door, we will enclose her with boards of cedar.
7:8.note: The brothers use architecture metaphors for their sister's readiness for marriage, with a silver battlement signaling honor and cedar panels suggesting protection.

7:9.rendering: I was a wall, and my breasts were like towers. Then I became in his eyes as one who finds peace.
7:9.note: The Hebrew word for "peace" here is shalom, echoing the covenant blessing of wholeness and rest found throughout the Hebrew Bible.

7:10.rendering: Solomon had a vineyard in Baal-hamon. He leased the vineyard to keepers. Each one was to bring for its fruit a thousand pieces of silver.
7:10.note: Baal-hamon means "lord of wealth," a clever wordplay that turns the vineyard into a symbol of lush abundance the king shares rather than hoards.

7:11.rendering: My own vineyard is before me. The thousand are for you, Solomon, and two hundred for those who keep its fruit.
7:11.note: This echoes the vineyard imagery of chapter 1:6 where the woman neglected her own vineyard, now she claims joyful ownership as the story resolves.

7:12.rendering: You who dwell in the gardens, with friends listening, let me hear your voice.
7:12.note: The Hebrew word for "dwell" echoes the garden imagery of Eden and the temple, inviting intimate communion where lovers' voices replace the first couple's hiding.

7:13.rendering: Flee away, my love, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the mountains of spices.
7:13.note: The Hebrew verb for "flee" echoes the urgent, wild chase of earlier garden scenes, inviting the lover to return swiftly like the deer he was compared to in chapter 2.

6:8.rendering: Your mouth is like the best wine: it flows smoothly for my beloved, gliding over lips and teeth.
6:8.note: The Hebrew word for "gliding" (or "flowing") echoes the same root used for the movement of the lover's body earlier in the chapter, turning her speech into an intoxicating continuation of her physical beauty.

6:9.rendering: I belong to my love, and his desire is for me.
6:9.note: The Hebrew word for "desire" here echoes the exact term in Genesis 3:16, but now transformed from curse to delight.

6:10.rendering: Come, my beloved, let us go out to the fields, let us spend the night among the henna blossoms.
6:10.note: The Hebrew word for "henna" plays on the sound of "to be gracious," echoing the woman's earlier plea for her lover to be like a gazelle on the spice-laden mountains.

6:11.rendering: Let us go early to the vineyards, let us see if the vine has budded, if its blossoms have opened, if the pomegranates are in bloom. There I will give you my love.
6:11.note: The repeated "let us see if" echoes the urgent inspection of growth in Song 6:11 and 2:12, turning a spring walk into a sensual quest for ripeness.

6:12.rendering: The mandrakes give off their fragrance, and at our doors are all choice fruits, new as well as old. I have stored them up for you, my beloved.
6:12.note: Mandrakes were ancient fertility charms, their strong scent signaling ripe desire in this garden of love.

5:0.rendering: Where has your beloved gone, most beautiful among women? Where has your beloved turned, that we may seek him with you?
5:0.note: The chorus of Jerusalem's daughters shifts from teasing to eager participation, echoing their earlier question in 5:9 and drawing the reader deeper into the lovers' shared pursuit.

5:1.rendering: My beloved has gone down to his garden, to the beds of spices, to feed in the gardens and to gather lilies.
5:1.note: The garden and lilies echo the lush imagery of Eden and the bride herself from earlier in the song.

5:2.rendering: I am my lover's and my lover is mine, the one who grazes among the lilies.
5:2.note: The mutual belonging echoes the covenant formula "you are mine and I am yours" from Hosea 2:23, turning romantic delight into a picture of faithful union.

5:3.rendering: You are beautiful, my love, as Tirzah, lovely as Jerusalem, awesome as an army with banners.
5:3.note: The Hebrew word for "awesome" here carries the sense of terrifying splendor, echoing how the beloved's beauty overwhelms like a battle formation on the horizon.

5:4.rendering: Turn your eyes away from me, for they overwhelm me. Your hair is like a flock of goats streaming down from Gilead.
5:4.note: The Hebrew verb here for "overwhelm" is the same one used for Rahab's terrorizing of Jericho's spies, revealing the lover's awe-struck vulnerability before beauty.

5:5.rendering: Your teeth are like a flock of ewes coming up from the washing, all of them bearing twins, and not one among them is bereaved.
5:5.note: The Hebrew word for "bereaved" echoes Rachel's lament in Genesis, turning a simple image of perfect symmetry into a whisper of restored fruitfulness.

5:6.rendering: Your temples behind your veil are like a slice of pomegranate. Your cheeks behind your veil are like halves of a pomegranate.
5:6.note: The Hebrew word for "temples" or "cheeks" (raqqah) echoes the same term used for Sisera's head in Judges 4 and 5, turning a violent image into one of intimate beauty.

5:7.rendering: There are sixty queens, and eighty concubines, and young women without number.
5:7.note: This ascending count of royal women echoes the scale of Solomon's actual harem in 1 Kings 11:3, quietly underscoring the singular devotion the bride still commands.

5:8.rendering: My dove, my perfect one, is only one. She is the only one of her mother, the favorite of the one who bore her. The young women saw her and called her blessed, the queens and concubines too, and they praised her.
5:8.note: The Hebrew word for "perfect one" echoes the earlier description of the garden in 4:7, linking her beauty to an unblemished Eden-like wholeness.

5:9.rendering: Who is this, looking down like the dawn, beautiful as the moon, bright as the sun, terrifying as an army with banners?
5:9.note: The Hebrew piles up four vivid similes in rapid succession, echoing the lover's awe from 6:4 while adding cosmic scale and holy fear.

5:10.rendering: I went down to the nut orchard to see the fresh growth in the valley, to see whether the vine had budded and the pomegranates had bloomed.
5:10.note: The lover descends into an orchard of walnuts, vines, and pomegranates, echoing the garden imagery that runs from Eden through the whole Bible.

5:11.rendering: Before I knew it, my desire set me among the chariots of my princely people.
5:11.note: The Hebrew is famously abrupt and obscure, like a sudden rush of overwhelming emotion that even the speaker can't quite explain.

5:12.rendering: Return, return, O Shulammite! Return, return, that we may gaze on you. Why should you gaze on the Shulammite as on the dance of two camps?
5:12.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "Shulammite" echoes both Solomon and the village of Shunem, while "the dance of two camps" alludes to the sacred dance of Mahanaim in Genesis 32.

0:0.rendering: The song of songs, which is Solomon's.
0:0.note: This title is a Hebrew superlative, the greatest song of all, setting the stage for an ancient love poem that echoes Eden's intimacy and points toward covenant joy.

0:1.rendering: Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth, for your love is better than wine.
0:1.note: The abrupt shift from "him" to direct "you" captures the ancient lover's longing in a single breathless line.

0:2.rendering: Your oils have a pleasing fragrance, your name is like the finest oil poured out, therefore the young women love you.
0:2.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "oil" (shemen) and "name" (shem), evoking how a person's reputation spreads like fragrant scent across a room.

0:3.rendering: Draw me after you, let us run. The king has brought me into his chambers. We will rejoice and be glad in you, we will remember your love more than wine. Rightly do they love you.
0:3.note: The Hebrew verb for "draw" echoes God's intimate leading of Israel out of Egypt in Hosea 11:4, turning a love song into an echo of covenant rescue.

0:4.rendering: I am dark but lovely, O daughters of Jerusalem, like the tents of Kedar, like the curtains of Solomon.
0:4.note: The Hebrew word for "dark" plays on the sunburned skin of a vineyard worker, contrasting with the luxurious royal curtains, an image of surprising beauty amid hard labor.

0:5.rendering: Do not stare at me because I am dark, because the sun has looked upon me. My mother's sons were angry with me, they made me keeper of the vineyards, but my own vineyard I have not kept.
0:5.note: The Hebrew word for "vineyard" plays on the woman's own body and beauty, an image that echoes through the whole song.

0:6.rendering: Tell me, you whom my soul loves, where you pasture your flock, where you make it lie down at noon. For why should I be like one who veils herself beside the flocks of your companions?
0:6.note: The Hebrew word for "veils herself" echoes the danger of being mistaken for a prostitute, a raw plea for belonging in this love song.

0:7.rendering: If you do not know, O most beautiful among women, follow the tracks of the flock, and pasture your young goats beside the shepherds' tents.
0:7.note: The Hebrew word for "tracks" echoes the same root used for the "way" or "path" of wisdom in Proverbs, inviting her into a shared journey of discovery.

2:0.rendering: On my bed at night I sought the one my soul loves. I sought him, but I did not find him.
2:0.note: The Hebrew word for "sought" echoes the urgent searching in Proverbs 8, where Wisdom cries out to be found at night.

2:1.rendering: I will rise now and go around the city, in the streets and in the squares. I will seek the one my soul loves. I sought him but did not find him.
2:1.note: The Hebrew word for "seek" (baqash) echoes the urgent search language of Proverbs 8, where Wisdom herself cries out in the streets to be found.

2:2.rendering: The watchmen found me as they went about the city. "Have you seen the one my soul loves?" I asked them.
2:2.note: In the ancient city, these watchmen were night patrols meant to guard order, yet here they become unexpected witnesses to the woman's lovesick search.

2:3.rendering: I had scarcely passed them when I found the one my soul loves. I held him fast and would not let him go until I had brought him into my mother's house, into the chamber of her who conceived me.
2:3.note: The Hebrew verb for "held him fast" echoes Jacob wrestling the angel until he received a blessing, turning the lover's embrace into a moment of tenacious, transformative encounter.

2:4.rendering: I charge you, daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles or by the does of the field: do not stir up or awaken love until it pleases.
2:4.note: The oath by wild gazelles and does evokes fragile, untamed beauty, urging restraint so love ripens in its own time.

2:5.rendering: Who is this coming up from the wilderness like columns of smoke, perfumed with myrrh and frankincense, with all the fragrant powders of the merchant?
2:5.note: The Hebrew word for "columns" echoes the pillar of cloud that led Israel through the wilderness in Exodus, turning a lovers' procession into an echo of God's dramatic rescue.

2:6.rendering: Look, it is Solomon's carriage, surrounded by sixty warriors, the best of Israel's fighting men.
2:6.note: The word for "carriage" (mittah) is the same used for a funeral bier in 2 Samuel 3:31, hinting that even royal processions carry echoes of mortality and procession toward the grave.

2:7.rendering: All of them are skilled with the sword, expert in war. Each one has his sword at his side, ready for the terrors of the night.
2:7.note: The word for "sword" (hereb) echoes the flaming sword guarding Eden in Genesis 3:24, turning paradise's lost defense into a bridal guard against night terrors.

1:0.rendering: I am a rose of Sharon, a lily of the valleys.
1:0.note: In the original Hebrew, the girl claims to be a common wildflower of the lowlands, not some exotic bloom, inviting her lover to see beauty in the ordinary.

1:1.rendering: Like a lily among thorns, so is my love among the daughters.
1:1.note: The Hebrew word for "thorns" (choach) evokes the cursed ground of Genesis 3:18, turning this into an image of unexpected beauty breaking through a broken world.

1:2.rendering: Like an apricot tree among the trees of the forest, so is my lover among the young men. In his shadow I delighted to sit, and his fruit was sweet to my taste.
1:2.note: The rare Hebrew word for "apricot" (or possibly apple) echoes the garden's forbidden fruit, turning a symbol of danger into one of delight and safety.

1:3.rendering: He brought me into the house of wine, and his banner over me was love.
1:3.note: The Hebrew word for "banner" evokes a military standard raised high in battle, turning a love song into an image of fierce, public protection.

1:4.rendering: Sustain me with raisin cakes, refresh me with apples, for I am faint with love.
1:4.note: The Hebrew verb for "faint" (or "sick") is the same one used for illness elsewhere, turning lovesickness into a recognized ancient malady that demands real nourishment.

1:5.rendering: His left hand is under my head, and his right hand embraces me.
1:5.note: The Hebrew word for "embraces" echoes the same verb used when God carries Israel in Deuteronomy 33:27, turning intimate human touch into an echo of divine care.

1:6.rendering: I charge you, daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles or by the does of the field: do not stir up or awaken love until it pleases.
1:6.note: The oath sworn by wild gazelles and does evokes their graceful, untamed freedom, a subtle wordplay on the beloved's name in Hebrew.

1:7.rendering: Listen! My lover! Here he comes, leaping across the mountains, bounding over the hills.
1:7.note: The Hebrew verbs for "leaping" and "bounding" echo the eager, gazelle-like movement described in verse 9, painting the beloved as a wild, untamed pursuer in this ancient love song.

0:8.rendering: I compare you, my love, to a mare among Pharaoh's chariots.
0:8.note: The word for "mare" (Hebrew susah) plays on the sound of "my love" (ra'yati), evoking how one stunning mare could throw an entire chariot team into chaos, an image of irresistible beauty in ancient royal stables.

0:9.rendering: Your cheeks are lovely with ornaments, your neck with strings of jewels.
0:9.note: The Hebrew word for "ornaments" echoes the tabernacle's lavish decorations, painting the beloved as a living sanctuary of beauty.

0:10.rendering: We will make earrings of gold for you, studded with silver beads.
0:10.note: The plural "we" hints at a community of admirers joining the king in adorning his beloved, echoing ancient Near Eastern wedding customs where friends and family lavished the bride with jewelry.

0:11.rendering: While the king was at his table, my spikenard sent forth its fragrance.
0:11.note: The Hebrew word for "spikenard" (nerd) echoes the scent filling the tabernacle in Exodus 30, turning this intimate moment into a whisper of sacred space.

0:12.rendering: My beloved is to me a sachet of myrrh that lies all night between my breasts.
0:12.note: In the ancient world, myrrh was a luxury spice used in love and burial, its scent lingering like a promise, an early echo of the fragrant offering that will one day be poured over Jesus' body.

0:13.rendering: My love is to me a cluster of henna blossoms from the vineyards of En-gedi.
0:13.note: The rare Hebrew word for "cluster" (eshkol) echoes the massive grape cluster the spies brought back from Canaan in Numbers 13, turning a desert oasis into a foretaste of abundance and delight.

0:14.rendering: Look at you, my love. Look at you, you are beautiful. Your eyes are doves.
0:14.note: The Hebrew piles up the word for "look" or "behold" twice for urgent delight, while "doves" evokes the soft, fluttering innocence of her gaze in ancient love poetry.

0:15.rendering: How beautiful you are, my love, how delightful! Our bed is lush with greenery.
0:15.note: The Hebrew word for "bed" here also means "couch of leaves," echoing the lush garden imagery of Eden and hinting at the Song's larger theme of restored paradise.

2:8.rendering: King Solomon made himself a palanquin from the wood of Lebanon.
2:8.note: The Hebrew word for this royal litter, appiryon, appears only here in the Bible, evoking the luxurious processions of ancient Near Eastern kings.

2:9.rendering: He made its posts of silver, its back of gold, its seat of purple. Its interior was inlaid with love by the daughters of Jerusalem.
2:9.note: The Hebrew for "inlaid with love" carries a double meaning of both affection and intricate craftsmanship, echoing how the tabernacle was filled with skilled, loving labor.

2:10.rendering: Come out and look, daughters of Zion, at King Solomon, at the crown with which his mother crowned him on the day of his wedding, on the day his heart rejoiced.
2:10.note: The Hebrew word for "crown" (atarah) echoes the same term used for the woman's garland of protection in 2:1-3, hinting at shared joy and honor in this wedding procession.

1:8.rendering: My beloved is like a gazelle or a young stag. Look, there he stands behind our wall, gazing through the windows, peering through the lattice.
1:8.note: The Hebrew word for "gazelle" (tsevi) echoes the name Zion, hinting at the lover's eager approach to his beloved's protected space, like a wild, graceful animal drawn to the city.

1:9.rendering: My beloved lifts up his voice and says to me, "Get up, my darling, my beautiful one, and come away."
1:9.note: The urgent double command "get up... come away" echoes God's calls to Abraham and Moses, turning romantic invitation into an exodus-like summons toward promise.

1:10.rendering: Look, the winter is past, the rain is over and gone.
1:10.note: The Hebrew word for "look" (hinneh) jolts the reader to attention, like a sudden glimpse of spring after the long, wet Judean winter.

1:11.rendering: The blossoms appear on the land, the time of the song has arrived, and the voice of the turtledove is heard in our land.
1:11.note: The Hebrew word for "song" (zamir) also means "pruning," so the verse layers the arrival of spring's music with the farmer's decisive cut, both signaling new life.

1:12.rendering: The fig tree ripens her early figs, and the vines in blossom give off their fragrance. Get up, my love, my beautiful one, and come away.
1:12.note: The Hebrew word for "ripen" (or "sweeten") echoes the same root used for "my love" earlier in the poem, linking the beloved's ripeness to the season's first sweet figs.

1:13.rendering: My dove, in the clefts of the rock, in the hidden places of the cliff, let me see your face, let me hear your voice, for your voice is sweet, and your face is lovely.
1:13.note: The Hebrew word for "clefts" (hagay) echoes the hiding place where Elijah heard God's still small voice in 1 Kings 19, turning this lover's invitation into an echo of divine pursuit.

1:14.rendering: Catch the foxes for us, the little foxes that ruin the vineyards, for our vineyards are in blossom.
1:14.note: The Hebrew word for "foxes" also means "jackals," sly scavengers whose stealthy raids on budding vines echo the subtle threats that can spoil love's early fruit.

1:15.rendering: My love is mine and I am his, the one who grazes among the lilies.
1:15.note: The Hebrew verb "graze" or "feed" echoes the shepherd imagery of earlier verses, painting their mutual belonging as a tender, shared pasture.

0:16.rendering: The beams of our house are cedars, our rafters are cypresses.
0:16.note: Cedars and cypresses, the prized woods of ancient palaces and temples, turn this simple shelter into a royal pavilion of love.

1:16.rendering: Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, turn, my love, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the cleft mountains.
1:16.note: The Hebrew word for "turn" echoes the urgent calls of Genesis 3:19 and 19:17, hinting at both longing and the fleeting nature of time.
